notes.note: A Roman calendar date: the Nones fell on the 5th of most months (the 7th in March, May, July, and October), so this is 5 April; Romans dated letters by kalends, nones, and ides.
Maiestas was the crime of treason or impairing the sovereign majesty of the Roman people; Appius, back from governing Cilicia, was prosecuted on this charge and acquitted in 50 BC.

The two standing criminal charges Appius faced: ambitus (electoral bribery) and maiestas (treason). Cicero's point is that an ambitus charge would at least imply real wrongdoing, whereas the maiestas charge was baseless.

notes.anchor: is what Sulla meant it to be
notes.note: The dictator Sulla's lex Cornelia de maiestate (81 BC) defined the offence broadly; Cicero says Sulla intended it precisely so that public men could not be assailed at will, the opposite of how it was used against Appius.
A flattering claim that the res publica, not merely individual advocates, had rallied to Appius's defence at his trial, treating his acquittal as a public necessity in a time of few capable leaders.
Appius, like Cicero, was an augur, and was composing a treatise on augural science (the Auguralis disciplina); Cicero asks him to set it aside for their shared leisure.

notes.anchor: as Aristarchus pronounces any line he does not approve to be not Homer's
notes.note: Aristarchus of Samothrace, the Alexandrian critic famous for marking (athetizing) Homeric verses he judged spurious; Cicero jokes that Appius may likewise disown any clumsy letter as not really his.
Appius's ancestor Appius Claudius Caecus, the celebrated censor of 312 BC (builder of the Appian Way and aqueduct), held up as the model Appius should emulate in his own censorship.
